coredata.dc:title: Novel applications of carbon dots (CDs) for enhanced preservation of postharvest fruit and vegetables

coredata.dc:description: Following harvest, fruit and vegetables experience natural aging processes, making them susceptible to biotic and abiotic stresses. Failure to adequately control these events results in substantial postharvest losses. Numerous technologies have been developed to address this issue, with nanotechnology emerging as a crucial solution. Its role in advancing fruit and vegetable packaging to extend shelf life and ensure food safety is particularly interesting. Recently, carbon dots have garnered significant interest, with ongoing exploration of their applications in postharvest fruit and vegetables yielding promising effects. This review aims to comprehensively elucidate recent developments and the potential usage of carbon dots in preserving harvested fruit and vegetables. The synthesis methods, characterization techniques, and applications of carbon dots directly on commodities or their incorporation in biodegradable films are summarized. Advanced technological approaches to prepare innovative carbon dot-based biodegradable films have also been introduced to stimulate new areas for future research. Incorporating nanoparticles into packaging materials has gained substantial attraction, showcasing the great potential for the commercial viability of active packaging. Integrating nanoparticles like carbon dots (CDs) developed from green technology and packaging materials can be a novel approach. Numerous functional groups characterize carbon dots, are non-toxic, are biocompatible, have potent antibacterial and antioxidative properties, are cost-effective, and have a feasible synthesis process that represents their advantageous usage. This review not only consolidates the existing knowledge but also provides a strategic roadmap for tailoring the positive attributes of CDs to meet specific requirements, particularly in the context of postharvest preservation and safety.

head.ce:title: Novel applications of carbon dots (CDs) for enhanced preservation of postharvest fruit and vegetables

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Following harvest, fruit and vegetables experience natural aging processes, making them susceptible to biotic and abiotic stresses. Failure to adequately control these events results in substantial postharvest losses. Numerous technologies have been developed to address this issue, with nanotechnology emerging as a crucial solution. Its role in advancing fruit and vegetable packaging to extend shelf life and ensure food safety is particularly interesting. Recently, carbon dots have garnered significant interest, with ongoing exploration of their applications in postharvest fruit and vegetables yielding promising effects. This review aims to comprehensively elucidate recent developments and the potential usage of carbon dots in preserving harvested fruit and vegetables. The synthesis methods, characterization techniques, and applications of carbon dots directly on commodities or their incorporation in biodegradable films are summarized. Advanced technological approaches to prepare innovative carbon dot-based biodegradable films have also been introduced to stimulate new areas for future research. Incorporating nanoparticles into packaging materials has gained substantial attraction, showcasing the great potential for the commercial viability of active packaging. Integrating nanoparticles like carbon dots (CDs) developed from green technology and packaging materials can be a novel approach. Numerous functional groups characterize carbon dots, are non-toxic, are biocompatible, have potent antibacterial and antioxidative properties, are cost-effective, and have a feasible synthesis process that represents their advantageous usage. This review not only consolidates the existing knowledge but also provides a strategic roadmap for tailoring the positive attributes of CDs to meet specific requirements, particularly in the context of postharvest preservation and safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fruit and vegetables are crucial in a nutritious diet due to their rich content of vitamins, pigments, minerals, antioxidants, fibers, etc., and they are on the top purchasing list of consumers. Although they are full of nutrition, their high moisture content (75–90%) makes them prone to rapid deterioration and postharvest decay, which leads to undesirable appearance and shorter shelf life (Otoni et al., 2017). These problems create a huge challenge for preserving fresh fruit and vegetables.

Packaging is crucial in the supply chain of fresh fruit and vegetables, from postharvest handling to the consumer plate, especially in the distribution network. It improves fruit quality and facilitates safety by creating a unified system for distribution, transportation, and marketing (Otto et al., 2021). According to the Food and Agricultural Organization reports, around one-third of fresh produce is lost during postharvest handling and transportation (Chen et al., 2021). Packaging helps to preserve fresh produce by shielding it from harmful environmental factors until it reaches the consumer. Conventional packaging cannot protect fruit and vegetables from postharvest deterioration and spoilage for longer. Besides, packaging does not meet the criteria of consumers' demand due to their smart purchasing priorities. Hence, postharvest losses, consumer demand, and awareness urged the need to overcome the issues of conventional packaging and the development of innovative packaging (Mohammadi & Jafari, 2020). The non-toxic and biocompatible nature of biopolymer-based packaging has gained more popularity. However, it does not fit the criteria of protecting fresh produce from microbial spoilage for a longer time, which hinders the safety concerns of consumers. Packaging materials can be incorporated with functional or active ingredients to ensure food safety and reduce deterioration. In this aspect, active packaging can be a suitable alternative for protecting the quality of fresh fruit and vegetablesfrom microbial contamination and preventing postharvest deterioration quality loss due to biochemical changes (Kuswandi et al., 2022). Numerous studies have indicated the successful application of various functional nanomaterials/particle in particles in developing active packaging films. Recently, the application of carbon dots(CDs) as nanoparticle and packaging materials has been trending due to their green synthesis technique and foods' beneficial effects on quality and shelf life.

Carbon dots represent a distinct class of nanomaterials characterized by their tiny size, i.e.; i.e., typically, less than 10 nm (Sun et al., 2006). The accidental discovery of carbon-based nanomaterials by Xu et al. (2004) ignited a wave of practical applications in various fields. Among all, CDs have drawn significant attention because of their distinct characteristics that include water solubility, cost-effectiveness, compatible usage, environmentally friendly, and low toxicity along with extensive functionalities (Fig. 1 .) like potent antioxidant, antibacterial activity, and light protection capabilities (Ejati et al., 2023). Beyond their biocompatibility and water solubility, they also possess other valuable properties such as adsorption interactions, tunable functional groups on the surface, and their diminutive size, which enables them to bind effectively to biological surfaces for applications in areas such as antimicrobial treatments (Ezati et al., 2022). As per the findings of Kumar et al. (2022), carbon dots synthesized from Enoki-mushroom were added in gelatine/carrageenan-based composite active packaging films, which enhanced the mechanical properties, antioxidant and UV blocking capabilities of films without hampering the transparency of films (Roy et al., 2021). Incorporating carbon dots enhanced films' antimicrobial properties (Li et al., 2022; Ma et al., 2020). Carbon dots prevented the spoilage caused by Penicillium digitatum (Liu et al., 2023). The inhibited microbial spoilage was also reported in in litchi (Mei et al., 2022) and eggplant (Nguyen et al., 2022).

This review aims to provide brief information about the synthesis of carbon dots, their antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, and their application in active packaging. Additionally, it provides an overview of the current studies on CDs and their application in active food packaging. The challenges of integrating CDs in active packaging films and future recommendations are also briefly discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods of synthesis of carbon dots from plant-based materials

ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal method is widely for carbon dots/quantum dots synthesis because of its user-friendliness and capacity to yield uniform-sized particles with high quantum yields (QY). Khan et al. (2023) employed the hydrothermal method to produce carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using garlic. In this process, 2 g of crushed garlic cloves were mixed with 50 ml of distilled water, sealed in a stainless-steel reactor within a Teflon-layered cylinder (100 ml), and subjected to an oil bath at 200 °C for 6 h. The resulting CQDs exhibited impressive UV-blocking capabilities (UV-A: 85.1% and UV-B: 99%). Furthermore, these CQDs demonstrated antibacterial activity against foodborne pathogens and strong antioxidant properties. Likewise, Khoskalampour et al. (2023) employed the hydrothermal method to synthesize CDs using grape leaves. The resulting CDs exhibited antibacterial effects against Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli. Deng et al. (2023) produced carbon dots from broccoli water extracts (BWE-CDs) via hydrothermal method. These BWE-CDs showed remarkable antioxidant activities due to the abundance of amino and carbonyl groups on their surfaces. Similarly, Researchers developed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from turmeric and banana peel using hydrothermal processing (Sul et al., 2023; Wen et al., 2023).

A microwave-assisted approach for synthesizing CQDs is simple, efficient and, cost-effective, facilitating rapid heat transfer to reactants, allowing swift reactions. Chandra et al. (2022) reported the green fluorescence of CQDs generated by mixing phosphoric acid and sucrose in a 100W microwave oven when kept for 3 min and 40 s. Zhu et al. (2013) pioneered the use of microwave methods to produce CQDs, achieving particles with a size of approximately 3.7 nm. Their process involved heating sugars and polyethylene glycol in a 500W microwave oven for 3 min. It resulted in CQDs that exhibited vivid blue fluorescence when checked under UV irradiation, boasting a quantum yield (QY) of 17%. Cao et al. (2018) produced the CQDs through the microwave-assisted method, using an aqueous glucose and arginine solution in a 700W microwave oven for approximately 10 min. The resulting CQDs exhibited diameters ranging from 1 to 7 nm. Furthermore, Architha et al. (2021) also harnessed microwave technology to create CQDs from mint leaves. They employed a 1200W microwave at varying durations (4, 5, 6, and 7 min). The resulting CQDs displayed notable behavior of excitation independence and radiated blue fluorescence, showcasing a remarkable fluorescence QY of 31%. Similarly, Malavika et al. (2021) worked on synthesizing CQDs from the aloe vera extract using a domestic LG microwave oven (80W). The CQDs generated in this process were amorphous, demonstrated an excitation-independent nature, blue fluorescence, and exhibited an impressive fluorescence QY of 31%.

Pyrolysis represents an irreversible heat deposition process characterized by degrading several materials or organic compounds under inert conditions. This method brings physicochemical transformations within organic materials under an inert atmosphere and synthesizes carbon-rich solid residue. Consequently, pyrolysis necessitates the utilization of precise pressure and very high temperature during its execution. A study conducted by Guerrero-Gonzalez et al. (2023) reported that CQDs were synthesized through the pyrolysis method employing agave fibers. During this process, 200 mg of agave powder was deposited on a hot plate and heated at 500 °C for varying durations, including 30 min, 1.0 h, and 2.0 h, to facilitate pyrolysis. Similarly, Tungare et al. (2020) pursued a parallel path by developing CQDs via the pyrolysis method, utilizing date palm fruit as a primary source. The resulting CQDs demonstrated notable antimicrobial properties against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, highlighting their potential in combating gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, highlighting their potential to combat harmful microorganisms.

Carbonization is an exceptionally cost-effective, convenient, and rapid one-step procedure for manufacturing CDs/CQDs. The chemical process involved in this method shows continuous pyrolysis in an inactive environment to generate solid materials with increased carbon content from organic sources.

Alex et al. (2020) used carbonization to synthesize CQDs from Lawsonia inermis leaves, commonly known as Henna. They employed UV–visible and luminescent spectroscopy techniques to characterize these environmentally friendly green carbon dotsto meticulously characterize these environmentally friendly green carbon dots meticulously. Moreover, they examined the surface morphology through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy.

In a complementary study, Swathi et al. (2021) developed a carbonization process assisted with microwaves and synthesized water-soluble fluorescent babul CDs from the seed powder. CDs obtained from babul seed were highly soluble in the water and exhibited more excitation-dependent emission. It also maintained better stability in a wide pH range and had higher ionic strength. Additionally, they demonstrated better resistance to photo bleaching.

CQDs from Buchnania lanzan leaf extract were synthesized inside an oven using the solvothermal method at 160 °C for 4 h (Jana & Dev, 2021). The UV–visible absorption spectra of the resulting CQDs exhibited characteristic peaks at 257 nm and 356 nm. Furthermore, the negative zeta potential and distinctive FT-IR signals provided evidence of functional groups (carbonyl, amine, and hydroxyl) present on the surface of CQDs. In addition, the XRD spectrum indicated that the CQDs possessed an amorphous structure featuring a prominent peak at 2θ = 19°.

Carbon dots possess inherent antimicrobial properties, making them effective against various diseases and microbial spoilage. These characteristics are influenced by various factors such as shape and size, charge on the surface, and the presence of different functional groups. Their antimicrobial activity involves photodynamic therapy, generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon illumination, damaging microbial cell membranes, DNA, and proteins; inducing oxidative stress by disrupting the balance between ROS production and antioxidant defenses; membrane disruption, altering structure and permeability, compromising cellular integrity; interfering with metabolic pathways by binding to essential enzymes and proteins; chelating essential metal ions, depriving microorganisms of necessary nutrients; and non-covalent interactions with microbial DNA, RNA, and proteins, disrupting genetic material and protein function (Khan et al., 2024; Lin et al., 2022). These mechanisms ultimately lead to cellular damage, apoptosis, or necrosis, demonstrating the potential of carbon dots as antimicrobial agents for various applications, including food packaging, wound healing, and water treatment (Hussen et al., 2024).

Li et al. (2022) introduced an application of CQDs in creating paper with antibacterial and waterproofing characteristics, primarily intended for food packaging to prolong the shelf-life of perishable foods. Their research demonstrated that the resulting material had strong antibacterial properties against gram-negative bacteria like E. coli and Listeria monocytogenes.

Besides, CQDs incorporated into Chitosan/gelatin-based composite films demonstrated significant antimicrobial activity against Aspergillus flavus and Colletotrichum orbiculare and exhibited significant antioxidant properties (Ezati et al., 2022). Similarly, Khan et al. (2023) found that including CQDs in the carrageenan/sodium alginate (Car/Alg) based composite films exhibits an antibacterial effect against L. monocytogenes and E. coli. Composite films with carbon dots synthesized from turmeric and sulfur represented good antimicrobial properties (maximum inhibition zone) due to higher free radical scavenging activity.

Fig. 3 illustrates various methodologies for characterizing Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs). These techniques encompass a range of analytical methods aimed at examining the properties and behavior of CQDs, providing insights into their structure, composition, and performance.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is used to assess the particle dimensions and crystalline characteristics of CDs/CQDs. XRD pattern of carbon quantum dots synthesized from aloe vera extract exhibited an amorphous nature (Malavika et al., 2021). Xu et al. (2020) observed that CQDs displayed an amorphous structure, as evidenced by a wide diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 20°. Likewise, Safardoust-Hojaghanet al. (2021) identified a broader peak at 2θ = 20° in the XRD pattern of CQDs produced from ZnO using microwave techniques. This peak broadening can be attributed to the formation of exceedingly small CQDs.

UV–visible spectroscopy is commonly used to assess the optical properties of CDs/CQDs. In this method, chemical molecules' absorption of ultraviolet or visible light indicates the different attributes of CDs/CQDs. Malavika et al. (2021) presented the UV–visible spectra for different reaction periods of CQDs, revealing the peak absorption at 301 nm. In a similar study by Yang et al. (2020), the highest absorption peak was observed at 301 nm, attributed to the electronic transition of the C=O bond (n-π∗). This transition was achieved through a hydrothermal process using Chitosan as source material. Pandiyan et al. (2020) observed a significant surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak in CD solutions centered on 295 nm. This peak results from the n-π∗ transition of the functional group C-OH or C=O bond, providing evidence of CD synthesis from garlic cloves. Moreover, Khan et al. (2023) reported UV–visible absorption spectra for CDs derived from garlic, covering a range from 200 to 600 nm.

The FT-IR technique analyzes a material's organic and inorganic functional groups. The functional groups on the surface of CDs/CQDs can affect the optical properties. MainlyCQDs' fluorescent characteristics prominently depend on their functional groups like OH, COOH, C=O, etc. (Tang et al., 2019). Several studies have reported that FTIR helped to differentiate the fluorescent properties of CDs/CQDs distinctively. For example, maximum broadband was observed at 3337 cm−1 and 1650 cm-1 using FTIR, which indicates the presence of -COOH, O-H, and C=O functional groups on the surface of CQDs (Boruah et al., 2020). Similarly, aromatic C-H bending was observed in FTIR at the fingerprint region 662 cm−1 (Zhu et al., 2021). Several studies confirmed that the FTIR technique facilitatedfacilitated the characterization of CDs/CQDs (Zhang et al., 2019) and prevented their accumulation during the post-synthetic process without any surface passivation (Jhonsi et al., 2018). Also, Wagh et al. (2023) reported the FTIR spectra signal peaks at 3301, 2962, 1579, 1384, and 1109 cm−1, which indicated the presence of O–H, C–H, C= C, N–H, and C=O groups, respectively. These findings confirmed the distinct structure of nitrogen (N–H group) present in the CDs, which was self-functionalized with other functional groups like hydroxyl (OH), carbonyl (CO), and amino groups (NH). These characteristics imparted excellent water solubility and bio-compatibility for further applications of CDs.

This technique is used to image CDs' internal structure through transmitting high-energy electrons. Wagh et al. (2023) observed the spherical structure (2.4–4.0 nm) and evenly distributed CDs in the multifunctional film using the TEM technique (Moradi et al., 2021). In this study, histograms made for particle size distribution indicated that the diameter of CDs varied from 2.4 to 4.0 nm with an average diameter of 3.2 nm. The CQDs derived from henna were analyzed by TEM, representing their spherical structure with an average diameter of 7–18 nm. The lattice fringes had 0.22 nm spacing due to the plane (1, 0, 0) of graphene (Alex et al., 2020). TEM images also showed that coffee-based CDs formed a stable suspension without aggregation, forming spherical and uniform-size CD particles (9.27–9.41 nm) in the quantum range (Min et al., 2022). They also described the appearance of CDs (particle size of 8.37 nm) and their dispersion ability without aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of biodegradable film incorporated with carbon dots/quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The use of synthetic polymers in commercial food packaging has become increasingly prevalent due to their cost-effectiveness and superior performance (Fig. 4 ). However, the environmental impact of these synthetic plastics raises significant concerns (Motelica et al., 2020). Therefore, adopting an appropriate biopolymer for producing active packaging films is crucial to enhance and maintain the fruit quality (nutritional value, color, textural properties, etc.) along with minimum or no microbial contamination. Incorporating CDs/CQDs in fruit and vegetable coatings or packaging has shown prominent improvements in microbial load prevention and better physic-chemical properties. In a study done by Mei et al. (2022), gelatin-based films incorporated with CDs synthesized from potato skin, exhibited higher UV- barrier properties and antioxidant activity than films without CDs. Similarly, CQDs synthesized from garlic cloves were subsequently used to fabricate carrageenan/sodium alginate (Car/Alg) functional film and facilitated UV-barrier properties. These Car/Alg-based films, when combined with 3 % CDs, effectively preserved the quality of packaged meat and prevented color changes until 30 h of storage at 20 °C (Khan et al., 2023).

Sul et al. (2023) synthesized CDs from banana peel to enrich the chitosan/gelatin-based film. This functional film, featuring CD-added chitosan/gelatin, was highly applicable in active packaging, particularly for enhancing the shelf life of highly perishable meat while preserving aesthetic appeal. The manufactured film showed higher antioxidant activity (>74% DPPH and 99% ABTS radical scavenging capacity). It exhibited significant antibacterial properties against Listeria monocytogenes, a foodborne pathogenic bacterium. Ejati et al. (2022) utilized glucose to synthesize CDs and incorporated it into chitosan/gelatin composite films. The incorporation of CDs significantly enhanced the film's UV blocking capability (99 %) without affecting the mechanical properties, water vapor permeability, and water contact angle. Moreover, the chitosan/gelatin/CDs-film coating substantially reduced mold formation on avocado fruit surfaces, extending the shelf life by more than 14 d.

Khoskalampur et al. (2022) used a casting method to produce carbon dot-based films using Persian gum and gelatin. Including CDs in these films reduced UV transmission rates and increased antioxidant activities (from 16.66 to 94.08%). Furthermore, the oxidized Persian gum/gelatin film containing CDs exhibited antibacterial properties against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. Films containing 30% CDs extended the shelf life of trout fillets by up to 4 d.

Similarly, Wen et al. (2023) developed a chitosan-based film infused with turmeric extract as carbon dots (hydrothermal technique). This approach involved mixing 1 g of chitosan with 100 mL of 1% acetic acid, followed by agitation at 45 °C for 4 h. Various concentrations of carbon dots were incorporated into the film, which was then dried and stored at room temperature. The resulting chitosan film exhibited improved mechanical properties, UV protection, and hydrophobicity due to CDs being included.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQDs on physical and mechanical properties of films
ce:section.ce:para: Table 2 summarizes the impact of incorporating carbon dots into composite films for active packaging. It outlines the observed effects and benefits of carbon dots in enhancing the properties and functionality of the packaging material.

The thickness of the produced film is a critical factor in determining its suitability as edible or active packaging material, and it is influenced by the amount of material used during the film development process. The incorporation of CDs showed no significant alteration within the polymer matrix or film thickness when small-sized CDs were used in low concentrations, when observed using FE-SEM images. Consequently, the uniform distribution of CDs in the film matrix contributes to preserving its thickness (Ejati et al., 2022). Riahi et al. (2022) also confirmed that the uniform dispersion of small-sized CQDs in the carboxy methyl cellulose film has no significant difference in thickness.

The fluorescence properties of CDs/CQDs have attracted research concern and facilitated their application in different areas, including bio-sensing, bio-imaging, delivery of drugs, and food packaging (Kumar et al., 2016). The fluorescence properties of CQDs arise from several mechanisms, namely, surface functionalization and size-dependent quantum confinement. This mechanism helps to optimize food packaging applications. The size of CDs confines quantum effects (Deka et al., 2022). Adjusting synthesis parameters like precursor concentration and reaction conditions can alter the particle size of CDs to obtain specific fluorescence emission. Smaller CDs generally have higher fluorescence intensity and emit light at shorter wavelengths (Zhang et al., 2019).

CDs derived from plant sources can be surface functionalized with several molecules or polymers as per the requirement of their fluorescence properties (Chen et al., 2019). Surface functionalization of CDs can enhance their stability, photoluminescence property, and selectivity intended for specific food packaging. It also improves quality control, tracking, and monitoring pathogens in the food packaging by selecting source and synthesis methods of CDs (Ezati et al., 2022). In this regard, using the hydrothermal method, Kumar et al. (2017) synthesized fluorescent CDs from basil leaves (Ocimum sanctum), which recorded higher stability in the water and showed strong fluorescence properties (quantum yield ∼9.3%).

Besides, CDs' fluorescence properties depend on the time and temperature used during synthesis. Li et al. (2018) synthesized CDs using a vegetable source (Hongcaitai) by the hydrothermal method (240 °C for 20 h), which reflected the prominent fluorescence properties. The study's findings indicated that very low temperatures or short reaction times affected the carbonization process of precursors, which led to lower quantum yield. However, a very high temperature or long reaction time decreased the surface functional groups and led to a low quantum yield.

Fluorescence properties also depend on CDs' solubility. CDs' solubility is marginal in polar solvents like ethanol, whereas it is highly soluble in water. Li et al. (2018) observed that ethanol-soluble CDs had a lesser quantum yield (17.8%) than water-soluble ones (21%).

The water solubility of film is a critical factor in determining the material's biodegradability. Cai et al. (2020) observed that the solubility of starch films containing thyme essential oil microcapsules (TEO-M) increased with a higher concentration of microcapsules than pure starch film. This phenomenon described the hydrophilic nature of the microcapsules. In contrast, Ejati et al. (2022) noticed a decrease in the water solubility of the film with an increasing concentration of CQDs. It indicated the reduced tendency of dissolving film into water due to incorporating CQDs. Similarly, Vargas-Torrico et al. (2022) reported that film solubility decreased upon including CDs (obtained from Hass avocado peel extract) in the gelatin/carboxymethylcellulose film. This reduction in solubility was attributed to enhanced interaction between the bio-polymeric matrix and the peel extract, which could have resulted in improved film integrity as a packaging material.

Barrier properties of films towards water vapor permeability (WVP) and oxygen are critical factors in deciding the quality and safety of food packaging materials. CDs are promising nano-fillers with great potential to enhance the barrier properties of food packaging materials for reducing water vapor and oxygen-related spoilage. The unique structural characteristics of CDs can be used to facilitate the barrier properties of packaging films to hinder the diffusion of water and oxygen molecules. CDs create a lengthy and complex route for water vapor transmission in thin films, which is a fundamental factor for lower water vapor. Besides, CDs have great potential to alleviate moisture-induced spoilage, extending packaged foods' shelf life. Better barrier strength of film maintains the textural properties, preserves taste and nutritional value by inhibiting the moisture loss and entry of oxygen inside the packages (Jafarzadeh et al., 2021).

Campalani et al. (2022) studied the addition of CDs into gelatin films, which enhanced the barrier properties of the composite film by 25% against water vapor. This observation considered that CDs made a tortuous and impermeable path, reducing the water vapor diffusion rate of water molecules across the films. Besides, CDs established cross-linking with gelatin and reduced the free spaces within the polymeric matrix resulting in lower water vapor permeability (WVP). Similarly, Kilic et al. (2022) reported that higher concentration of CDs in the gelatin-based films exhibited a 58.18 % decrease in WVP. Results indicated that for a highly hydrophilic protein-based film, significant levels of cross-linking are required for a pronounced reduction in the WVP. Hence, this reduction in WVP could be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between CDs and the gelatin. It also decreased the availability of hydrophilic sites needed for water vapor attachment and subsequent permeation. In another study, adding CDs in cellulose nano-fiber-based composite film reduced the oxygen transmission rate from 5.08 to 0.01 cc/m2 per day. This difference was attributed to the hydrophilic nature of CDs. The film's oxygen barrier properties might be enhanced due to the denser structure formed by synergistic interfacial bonding between carbon dots and film polymers (Ezatiet al., 2022).

In contrast, Sul et al. (2023) found that increasing the concentration of CQDs derived from banana peel in packaging films had no significant effect on the WVP of the film. It suggested that even a small amount of CQDs (3 %wt) had a minimal impact on WVP in chitosan/gelatin films. Additionally, Ezati et al. (2022) reported that low levels (2%wt) of CQDs in agar-based films had little effect on WVP, while increasing concentration of CQDs increased WVP. However, carrageenan/alginate-based functional films incorporated with CDs had a non-significant impact on WVP reduction (Khan et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQDS on mechanical properties of film
ce:section.ce:para: Table 3 provides an overview of how including Carbon Dots (CDs) or Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) affects the film's physical and mechanical properties. It outlines the alterations and enhancements observed in parameters such as flexibility, tensile strength, transparency, and barrier properties due to incorporating CDs/CQDs into the film matrix.

Tensile strength (TS) is a fundamental mechanical property of packaging materials, which determines the material's ability to withstand stress during packaging, transportation, and distribution. Deshmukh et al. (2022) observed a significant improvement in the film's tensile strength by adding bael shell extract (BSE) and TiO2. This enhancement can be attributed to the synergistic effect of TiO2 and BSE. Similarly, Ezati et al. (2022) reported a similar outcome in chitosan/gelatin-based films incorporating CQDs, which led to a slight increase in the tensile strength of the chitosan/gelatin film. The study by Khan et al. (2023) involved carrageenan/alginate (Car/Alg) based functional film incorporating garlic carbon dots. The study's findings indicated that the tensile strength of the Car/Alg film increased significantly upon the addition of CDs. Adding 4% CD, the tensile strength of the Car/Alg film reached 58.6 MPa, representing a 28% increase compared to the normal Car/Alg films. This enhancement was attributed to the hydrogen bonding interactions between distinct functional groups of CDs and the Car/Alg polymer matrix. Sul et al. (2023) also found a similar outcome in chitosan/gelatin-based films by increasing the concentration of CQDs derived from banana peel.

The mechanical properties of films are of utmost importance. Packaging materials must possess satisfactory mechanical strength and integrity to prevent breakage or leakage during storage and distribution (Ezati & Rhim, 2021). Sul et al. (2023) observed that increasing the concentration of CQDs derived from banana peel significantly reduced film elongation at break (EAB). The introduction of CQDs into the film enhanced strength by interacting with the polymer, but it simultaneously limited the molecular mobility of the polymer strands, resulting in reduced flexibility. Similarly, Ezati et al. (2022) reported a similar outcome in chitosan/gelatin-based films containing CQDs, where the inclusion of CDs resulted in a decline in EAB value of the chitosan/gelatin (Chi/Gel) film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQDs on antimicrobial properties of film
ce:section.ce:para: CDs from diverse sources demonstrate antimicrobial properties against various foodborne and spoilage-causing microbes. The extent and effectiveness of CDs antimicrobial activity vary with their size, shape, surface charges, and functional groups (Das et al.,2014). The molecular mechanisms by which CDs confer antibacterial properties involve: 1. Destruction of cell wall 2. CDs interaction with nucleus components 3. Production of reactive oxygen species (Kousheh et al., 2020). Table 4 outlines the mechanisms underlying Carbon Quantum Dots' (CQDs) inhibitory action against bacteria and fungi, while Table 5 summarizes the effects of CQDs on postharvest spoilage-causing pathogens.

The CDs/CQDs interact with the phospholipid bi-layer and cause roughening and shrinkage of the cell membrane. Moreover, direct attachment of CDs/CQDs leads to cell rupture, which causes the leakage of cellular components. The electrostatic interaction of CDs/CQDs positive charges with negative charges of cellular components leads to membrane stresses in the cell wall. Dealing metal/ions into the cell increases the toxicity level, resulting in cell death.

Some CQDs can move inside and accumulate in the nucleus, inhibiting cellular respiration, cell division, replication, and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) generation by interaction with nuclear material. This interaction finally causes cell apoptosis and damages the nucleus.

Due to higher electron transfer, CDs/CQDs produce huge quantities of free electrons and holes. The photo-activation produces excessive free radicals, such as per-hydroxyl anions, superoxide singlet oxygen (O2−), triplet oxygen, and hydroxide ion (OH−). The ROS accumulation inside cell inhibits cellular respiration and reproduction, causing reproduction and cell death of microbes.

Carbon dots use electrostatic interactions and diffusion to break down the cell walls of bacteria and fungi (Fig. 5 ). Bacterial physiological metabolism is disrupted by the electrostatic effects that stop them from growing or ingesting nutrients (Zhao et al., 2021). Alternately, CQDs enriched films were reported to disrupt bacterial cell membranes, causing leakage in the cellular cytoplasm and apoptosis of bacteria (Wang et al., 2019). Additionally, CDs had permeability to cell membranes of microbes and bound to DNA and RNA through non-covalent interactions, which altered the molecule structure and caused distortion of DNA double-helix. Eventually, the antibacterial effect of CDs resulted from several factors that influenced the proliferation of bacteria (Pandiyan et al., 2020). According to Gagic et al. (2020), CDs are harmless to normal cells and have good biocompatibility. Ma et al. (2020) synthesized CDs using biomass, which showed a broad-spectrum antibacterial effect. When the dosage increased to 1000 μg/ml, the CDs demonstrated potent antibacterial action against E. coli and S. aureus. In this study, CDs-treated E. coli and S. aureus showed cell wall damage and cytoplasmic leakage in a scanning electron microscope. However, ROS produced by CDs has a photodynamic effect on cancer cells. It was noticed that antibacterial activity and ROS production were directly correlated (Wang et al., 2014). CDs are attached to bacterial surfaces and modify the cell membrane structure/permeability, as shown in Fig. 4. On the other hand, photo-excitation of CDs by visible light effectively activated oxygen in the air or water to produce ROS (OH• or O2). According to Yang et al. (2016), ROS causes oxidative damage to vital intracellular macromolecules, which alters or inhibits the expression of vital genes and ultimately results in cell death.

Antimicrobial activity is a crucial functional feature in active food packaging. Deshmukh et al. (2022) found that films containing bael shell extract and TiO2 exhibited significant antibacterial activity against L. monocytogenes and E. coli, indicating their potential for inhibiting microbial growth. Using the agar well diffusion method, Ezati et al. (2022) qualitatively examined the inhibitory effect of CQDs-added films against various microorganisms, including L. monocytogenes, E. coli, A. flavus, and C. orbicular. CQDs in the films resulted in inhibitory zones against these test microorganisms, and the extent of inhibition depended on the concentration of CQDs. Similarly, Khan et al. (2023) found that depending on the concentration, adding CDs to the carrageenan/sodium alginate (Car/Alg) based composite films exhibits antibacterial activity against L. monocytogenes and E. coli. Meanwhile, the normal Car/Alg film showed no antibacterial activity. Fan et al. (2019) demonstrated that coating fresh-cut cucumbers with chitosan coatings containing CDs at different concentrations improved the storage quality of cucumbers. This coating prevented the growth of mold and yeast colonies during storage, indicating the antimicrobial properties of CDs. Additionally, Roy et al. (2020) reported that CDs-added chitosan/gelatin films exhibited an inhibition zone against test microbes, and the extent of inhibition depended on the concentration of CDs. Similarly, adding avocado peel extract (APE) significantly reduced fungal growth in gelatin/carboxymethyl cellulose active films. APE exhibited a lethal effect against R. stolonifer and A. niger and enhanced the shelf life of food by 5 day at 25 °C. This study demonstrated the potential of APE as an antimicrobial agent in food packaging (Vargas-Torrico et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of carbon dots added films on fruit and vegetables
ce:section.ce:para: In packaging fresh produce, edible coatings and films become highly popular as they offer biodegradable and environmentally friendly alternatives to conventional plastic packaging. This technique provides a better option in response to the growing environmental consciousness among consumers, who increasingly prioritize eco-friendly packaging solutions. These edible materials are widely used for packaging fruit, vegetables, and other food products. Unlike traditional plastic packaging, edible packaging serves as a specialized barrier, effectively controlling the diffusion of water and oxygen. This unique characteristic significantly extends the shelf life of fresh produce. In today's consumer preference, there is a huge demand for food that ensures safety and provides higher nutritional value, diversity, and longer storage period. Studies have demonstrated that adding carbon dots to the film increases opacity without affecting mechanical properties, water vapor permeability, and water contact angle (WCA).

The film incorporated with CQDs prevents spoilage and maintains the quality of fruit and vegetables by reducing moisture loss, microbial infection, and an upsurge in ethylene and respiration rate. Fan et al. (2019) demonstrated that mold and yeast development could be effectively prevented using a chitosan-based film containing 4.5% CQDs for up to 15 days of storage. Roy et al. (2021) fabricated active food packaging films from Enoki mushrooms, utilizing gelatin and carrageenan as key components. These films exhibited enhanced physical and functional attributes, making them suitable for active packaging to extend fresh produce's shelf-life. This improvement in the functionality of films and physicochemical properties of food was attributed to CQDs. Another noteworthy study by Abu and Gedanken (2021) reported an enhanced appearance and longer shelf-life of strawberries treated with chitosan nanoparticle-based edible coating. Arshad et al. (2020) developed a ginger oil-enriched corn starch-based coating that prevents lipid oxidation in food products. Coating fresh-cut papaya with Aloe vera gel (AVG) has been reported to prevent weight loss and firmness and extend shelf life when stored at 28 °C (Mendy et al., 2019).

Furthermore, Ezati et al. (2022) found that chitosan/gelatin-based film integrated with CQDs as a coating on avocado could reduce mold growth on the fruit surface and extend shelf life by 14 d. Nguyen et al. (2022) studied the impact of passion fruit peel pectin and chitosan (P/CH) based biodegradable films that were enriched with Piper betle (PB) leaf extract. Using P/CH/PB films yielded highly favorable outcomes for purple brinjal, including outstanding antibacterial activity, positive effects on total soluble solids, a reduction in water loss, and an extended shelf life of up to 15 d.

Similarly, Odjo et al. (2022) used a chitosan-based antimicrobial film specially designed with encapsulated lemon essential oil (LEO) and cranberry juice. Incorporating LEO and cranberry juice into the chitosan films brought notable improvements, such as improved UV barrier properties, enhanced thermal characteristics, and higher antioxidant activity. These advancements effectively combated fungal organisms like Candida albicans and Penicillium roqueforti.

Vargas-Torrico et al. (2022) worked on preserving berries using active films composed of gelatin and carboxyl methylcellulose enriched with Hass avocado peel extract. These films exhibited several beneficial characteristics, including the maintenance of thermal properties, regulation of moisture levels, reduction in film solubility, and preservation of berry fruit for a duration of up to six days. They also demonstrated effectiveness against fungal pathogens such as R. stolonifer and A. niger. In another study, Ma et al. (2022) introduced a composite film made from chitosan and Paeoniasuf fruticosa Leaf extract, which was applied in sustainable active apple packaging. This composite film played a pivotal role in enhancing thermal stability and reducing the browning of chopped apples by improving the barrier against water permeation.

Similarly, Chaudhary et al. (2021) developed a packaging film utilizing leftover garlic peel extract and chitosan. This development led to increased thickness, improved tensile strength, and enhanced thermal stability of the packaging material. Consequently, it facilitated the retention of higher moisture levels and preserved the antioxidant qualities of various fruit and vegetables. Furthermore, Tanwar et al. (2021) ventured into creating an edible film for packaging ber (Ziziphus mauritiana) fruit. Their approach involved microwave-assisted mint leaf extract and citric acid (GGM/CA). This edible coating extended the storage life and maintained the quality of the fruit for up to 15 days at 25 °C and 75% relative humidity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQDs-based film on physical and biochemical properties of fruit and vegetables

ce:section.ce:para: The weight loss of fruit is closely associated with water loss, a crucial quality criterion that directly impacts its marketability and shelf life. Weight loss is primarily attributed to continuous respiration, evaporation, and environmental moisture transmission through the fruit's outer skin (Duan et al., 2011). The weight loss was significantly lower in litchi fruit packed in films made from silk sericin chitosan integrated with carbon dots compared to the control (Mei et al., 2022). Furthermore, fruit coated with a Chitosan containing 4.5% CDs had the lowest weight loss (7.82%) than normal chitosan coating (Riahi et al., 2022).

The color of fruit and vegetables is crucial, impacting their visual appeal, marketability, and consumer acceptance. This change is due to various pigments like chlorophyll, carotenoids, anthocyanin, lycopene, and anthoxanthin. These pigments affect color and provide antioxidants with potential health benefits. Peel color alterations involve chlorophyll degradation and changes in green pigments, leading to the development of other pigments (Salvador et al., 2007). This dynamic color transformation greatly influences the visual appeal and desirability of mangoes among consumers.

Fruit firmness is a crucial quality parameter that directly affects the edible quality of fruit. The application of CQDs-based film helps retain fruit firmly during storage. Cai et al. (2020) studied the effect of starch-based films containing thyme essential oil on the physico-chemical attributes of mango. This film created a low-oxygen environment on the mango surface, which inhibited the activity of softening enzymes, such as pectinase. It prevented the disintegration of fruit tissues' cell walls and other structural components. Thus, mango fruit packed in carboxymethyl chitosan-pullulan (CMCS/Pull) films experienced a reduction in softening. Mei et al. (2022) found a similar trend in fresh-cut cucumber, with control samples experiencing a more substantial decrease in firmness compared to samples coated with 4.5% carbon dots/chitosan (CDs/CH) during a 15-day storage period. This study suggested that the CDs/CH coating acted as a barrier to delay softening process, likely due to reduced oxygen availability and a lower metabolic rate within the coated samples.

The total soluble solids (TSS) content of fruit is a critical parameter that significantly impacts fruit quality and consumer acceptance. Mei et al. (2022) observed that litchi fruit packed in functional films containing silk sericin chitosan integrated with carbon dots (SS/CS/CDs) exhibited lower TSS levels than control fruit. This indicated that the CDs added films helped retaining higher TSS during storage.

Titratable acidity is a crucial component that contributes to the flavor profile of fruit. A study on CQDs-incorporated films revealed that these functional films have positive results in the reduction of titratable acidity in fruit during storage. Mei et al. (2022) reported that composite film consisting of silk sericin, chitosan, and carbon dots (SS/CS/CDs) significantly inhibited the decrease in TA of litchi fruit.

Phenolic compounds, abundant in various fruits and vegetables, are vital in the human diet, potentially reducing the risk of cardiovascular diseases and certain cancers due to their antioxidant properties (Duan et al., 2007). However, these compounds are prone to oxidation by enzymes like PPO (polyphenol oxidase) and POD (peroxidase) during storage (Zhang et al., 2000). Coating litchi fruit with aloe vera gel increased total phenols by 1.66-fold on the 8th day of storage compared to fruit without coating (Ali et al. (2019). Similarly, mango fruit treated with chitosan showed higher retention of phenolic compounds (Wang et al., 2023).

Packaging food with antioxidant-releasing films effectively inhibits the oxidation of nutrients and increases the shelf life. Many types of radicals, such as 2,2-diphenyl-1-picryl-hydrazyl-hydrate radicals (DPPH), hydroxyl radicals (-OH), KMnO4 radicals, and superoxide's anion radicals, raise the antioxidant activity of CQDs. The antioxidant activity of fruit and vegetables is primarily attributed to the scavenging activity of free radicals and transition metal ions. The gelatine/CDs-based film shows high antioxidant activity and inhibits deterioration of packed foods sensitive to oxidative deterioration. The protective ability of CDs is attributed to structural characteristics of film surface, which scavenge free radicals (ABTS and DPPH methods) from the product. Sun et al. (2021) studied the effect of sodium alginate-based nanocomposite films incorporated with CQDs (kiwi peel), which had strong antioxidant properties that enhanced the effectiveness of natural antioxidants in banana fruit. A study reported that antioxidant activity in gelatine film was 27 % while incorporating CDs (2 and 4 %) enhanced the scavenging capacity (ABTS method) of the film by 99 and 99.6%, respectively (Min et al., 2022). From the above results, it was concluded that the antioxidant activity of CD-incorporated film was higher in ABTS method than in the DPPH method. It could be due to the higher expansion rate of gelatine film in the aqueous ABTS mixture compared to the methanolic solution of DPPH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs on antimicrobial properties of film
ce:section.ce:para: Numerous studies demonstrate that incorporating CDs into packaging films can enhance antimicrobial activities against various pathogens. The gelatine-based film added with carbon dots showed antibacterial activity against E. coli (Kousheh et al., 2020). Composite films inhibited the growth of E. coli and L. monocytogenes and recorded higher antibacterial activity when higher concentrations of CDs were added. Similarly, functional films added with CDs derived from turmeric and sulfur compounds showed higher antimicrobial activities. In this study, the zone inhibition method was used to determine the antimicrobial activity of CDs (Roy et al., 2020). CDs can also be used in biopolymer solutions for the antibacterial coating of foods. Fan et al. (2019) reported that CDs-based coating on fresh-cut cucumber enhanced its antimicrobial activities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges and limitations of carbon dots incorporated packaging films
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots incorporated packaging films have gained significant attention for fresh produce preservation due to their unique optical, electrical, and chemical properties. However, several challenges and limitations hinder their widespread adoption. One major challenge is ensuring uniform dispersion and stability of carbon dots in the polymer matrix during film processing. This affects the film's optical and mechanical properties. Scalability is another issue, as large-scale production of high-quality carbon dots and their incorporation into packaging films is essential for commercial viability. Potential toxicity and biocompatibility concerns related to carbon dots also need thorough investigation. Regulatory frameworks for carbon dot-based packaging materials are lacking, hindering their adoption.

The high production costs of carbon dots and modified packaging films must be reduced for industrial applications. Moreover, carbon dots' sensitivity to moisture affects their performance and stability in humid environments. Their optical properties may degrade over time or under specific conditions, reducing effectiveness. Chemical resistance is also a concern, as carbon dots may not withstand certain chemicals. Temperature stability is another limitation, as extreme temperatures impact carbon dots' performance.

Despite these challenges, researchers are exploring solutions. Developing hybrid materials combining carbon dots with other nanomaterials or additives enhances performance. Surface modification improves dispersion, stability, and biocompatibility. Scalable synthesis methods for high-quality carbon dots are being developed. Toxicity assessments and regulatory frameworks are essential for widespread adoption. Future research should focus on addressing these challenges and limitations. This includes investigating recyclability and environmental impact, exploring alternative synthesis methods, and conducting thorough toxicity assessments. Overcoming these hurdles will unlock the potential of carbon dots incorporated packaging films for fresh produce preservation.

Green synthesis of CDs/CQDs and their desired functional features have drawn massive attention toward its utility for active packaging. This article comprehensively analyzed the use of CDs/CQDs in food packaging and emphasized the current advancements in this field. Commercializing packaging techniques is anticipated to enhance food safety and significantly extend the shelf life of fresh produce. Technologies based on active packaging are crucial for preserving food quality and boosting food safety. CDs as functional materials can maintain the quality and enhance the shelf life of fruit and vegetables. Antimicrobial properties of CDs help to reduce spoilage throughout distribution and storage of fresh produce. Synthesizing CDs from horticultural crops is eco-safe and has no residual effects. Researchers are investigating and synthesizing CDs from sustainable biomass for diverse uses including application of CDs in the preservation of fruit and vegetables as an eco-friendly option. Recent studies emphasize the beneficial qualities of CDs and their enormous potential in food packaging sectors as well. However, there are miles to go to achieve stable, scalable, and applicable CDs/CQDs, warranting further research.
